metadata.title: Research the Warburg Effect: Is Aerobic Glycolysis a Therapeutic Vulnerability?

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Sawubona, Liam — in for Bear. Warburg — forty years of trying? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.segment: Research the Warburg Effect: Is Aerobic

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Warburg — forty years of trying.

The PET scan glows where cancer is because tumors consume glucose at ten times the rate of normal tissue. Forty years of attempts to drug this have mostly failed. The question is why — and why IDH inhibitors are the narrow exception that proves the rule about therapeutic windows.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- problem card: PET scan glucose uptake; forty years of failed attempts

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to research the clinical history of Warburg-targeting drug attempts and contrast with IDH inhibitors.

props.command:
claude "Research clinical attempts to target the Warburg
effect: 2-deoxyglucose, dichloroacetate, HIF-1alpha
inhibitors. For each: the most advanced clinical
trial, reported response rates, and the stated
reason for failure. Then contrast with IDH
inhibitors (ivosidenib, enasidenib) -- what made
those succeed when broader Warburg targeting did not?"

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) -- Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the code. The script builds a drug-attempt timeline from Claude's response — columns for drug, mechanism, best trial, ORR, and outcome. Sorting by ORR shows the pattern immediately.

props.code:
# warburg_timeline.py -- Claude Code output
DRUGS = [
  {"drug": "2-Deoxyglucose", "mech": "GLUT/HK block",
   "trial": "Phase I/II", "orr": "<5%",
   "outcome": "Toxicity; universal target"},
  {"drug": "Dichloroacetate", "mech": "PDK inhibitor",
   "trial": "Phase II (GBM)", "orr": "<10%",
   "outcome": "No survival benefit"},
  {"drug": "HIF-1a inhibitor", "mech": "HIF block",
   "trial": "Phase II", "orr": "<10%",
   "outcome": "Off-target toxicity"},
  {"drug": "Ivosidenib", "mech": "IDH1 inhibitor",
   "trial": "Phase III", "orr": "41%",
   "outcome": "FDA APPROVED (AML)"},
]
print(f"{'Drug':<20} {'ORR':<8} Outcome")
for d in sorted(DRUGS, key=lambda x: x['orr'], reverse=True):
  print(f"{d['drug']:<20} {d['orr']:<8} {d['outcome']}")

beats.narration_text: The timeline table. 2-DG, dichloroacetate, HIF inhibitors all showing under ten percent ORR or discontinued due to toxicity. IDH inhibitors showing over forty percent ORR and FDA approval. The separation is visible: broad Warburg targeting fails, narrow metabolic defect targeting succeeds.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- Remotion timeline to be built: drug attempts on horizontal axis with ORR annotations

beats.narration_text: Now check the current landscape: which trials are showing the best response rates?

props.command:
claude "Which current clinical trials targeting
aerobic glycolysis show the highest response
rates? Does any look like it might replicate
the IDH inhibitor pattern of narrow success?"

beats.narration_text: Current trials. IDH2 inhibitor enasidenib showing thirty to forty percent response in IDH2-mutant AML. GLUT1 inhibitors and hexokinase inhibitors still in Phase 1 with no signal yet. The pattern holds: where a tumor-specific metabolic defect exists, the therapeutic window opens. Where the Warburg effect is universal, no window.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- Remotion timeline to be built: same axis extended with current trials

beats.narration_text: The Warburg effect is real but too universal — every proliferating cell uses it. The therapeutic window narrows to zero for broad glycolysis inhibitors because normal bone marrow and gut epithelium depend on the same pathway. Success requires a tumor-specific metabolic defect — which is exactly what IDH mutations provide.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: universal vs tumor-specific metabolic target; IDH as narrow exception

beats.narration_text: Look up whether your cancer type has an IDH mutation status test as standard of care — that is the narrow metabolic handle that has produced an approved drug. AML and glioma are the two types. If neither applies, the Warburg effect is not currently a therapeutic target.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps: check IDH status for AML and glioma patients

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The Warburg effect is real but too universal — every proliferating cell uses it. The therapeutic window narrows to zero for broad glycolysis inhibitors because normal bone marrow and gut epithelium depend on the same pathway. Success requires a tumor-specific metabolic defect — which is exactly what IDH mutations provide. Look up whether your cancer type has an IDH mutation status test as standard of care — that is the narrow metabolic handle that has produced an approved drug. AML and glioma are the two types. If neither applies, the Warburg effect is not currently a therapeutic target.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
